title: How to Fix a Garage Door That Won't Open or Close
question: My garage door won't open or close — what should I check first?
summary: Start simple: check power and the GFCI outlet, the remote and keypad batteries, and — for a door that won't close — the two photo-eye sensors near the floor, since a blocked or misaligned sensor is the most common cause. Sensor, battery, and lock issues are easy DIY. A broken spring or cable, or a door off its track, is a pro job — those parts are under extreme tension. Plan on 10-40 minutes for the safe checks.

commonCauses.cause: Photo-eye safety sensors blocked, dirty, or misaligned — the door reverses or won't close and the opener light often blinks
commonCauses.quickCheck: Look at the two small sensors about 6 inches off the floor on each side of the door. Clear anything in the beam and check the indicator LEDs — one steady, one usually blinking when misaligned. The opener light flashing a set number of times is the classic sensor signal.

commonCauses.cause: Power or remote problem — opener unplugged, GFCI tripped, dead remote/keypad battery, or the wall lock-out button engaged
commonCauses.quickCheck: Does the hardwired wall button work but the remote doesn't? Replace the remote battery. Does nothing work? Check that the opener is plugged in and the outlet has power (reset the GFCI). Check for a 'lock' button on the wall console that's been held/engaged.

commonCauses.cause: Broken torsion spring or lift cable, or the door off its track — the door is very heavy by hand, crooked, or you see a gap in the coiled spring above the door
commonCauses.quickCheck: With the opener disconnected (pull the red release), try lifting the door by hand — a properly balanced door lifts easily and stays put halfway. If it's extremely heavy, slams down, looks crooked, or the spring above the door has a visible gap, STOP — this is a high-tension repair for a pro.

fixes: Check power. Confirm the opener is plugged in and the ceiling outlet has power — reset the GFCI outlet or breaker if it's tripped. Many 'totally dead' openers are just an unplugged unit or a tripped GFCI.

Rule out the remote vs. the wall button. If the wall-mounted button opens the door but the remote/keypad doesn't, replace the remote and keypad batteries; re-program the remote if a battery swap doesn't fix it. If even the wall button does nothing, it's power or the opener itself, not the remote.
Check the wall console lock-out. Many wall consoles have a 'lock' or vacation button that disables the remotes — make sure it isn't engaged.

For a door that won't CLOSE, fix the photo-eyes. Wipe both sensor lenses, clear the beam path, and gently bend/adjust the brackets until both LEDs glow steady (not blinking). Sensors slightly knocked out of alignment are the #1 won't-close cause. Loose or pinched sensor wiring can do it too.
Check the manual lock and the track. Make sure a manual slide-lock on the door isn't thrown. Look for an obvious obstruction, a roller jumped out of the track, or a bent track section.

Test the balance safely. Pull the red emergency-release cord to disconnect the door from the opener, then lift the door by hand. It should glide and stay open about halfway. If it's heavy or won't stay, the spring is failing — reconnect, leave it down, and call a pro.

Re-engage the opener. After manual checks, pull the release cord back or run the opener to re-latch the trolley, and test a full open/close cycle while watching that it reverses on the sensors and doesn't bind.
Do NOT attempt to adjust, loosen, or replace the torsion spring or lift cables. They store enormous energy and can cause severe injury — this is a pro repair, always.
diyVsPro: Power, GFCI, remote/keypad batteries, lock-out buttons, photo-eye cleaning/alignment, and clearing an obstruction are safe DIY. Call a garage-door pro for: a broken or gapped torsion spring, frayed or snapped lift cables, a door off its track or visibly crooked, a bent track, or any door that's extremely heavy to lift by hand. Spring and cable work is the leading cause of serious garage-door injuries and requires special winding bars and training — never DIY it.
typicalCostUSD: DIY: $5-15 for remote/keypad batteries; $0 for sensor alignment, GFCI reset, and lock-out fixes. Pro: torsion-spring replacement roughly $150-350 (often done in pairs), cable replacement $100-200, off-track or roller repair $125-250, a new opener installed $300-600+.
timeEstimate: 10-40 minutes for the DIY checks (power, remote, sensors, lock, balance test).
safetyNote: The garage door is the heaviest moving object in most homes, and its torsion spring and cables are under extreme tension. The DIY checks here — power, batteries, sensor alignment, lock-out, and a hands-off balance test using the red release cord — are safe. But NEVER loosen, adjust, or replace the torsion spring, the cables, or the bottom-bracket bolts: a released spring can break bones or worse. If the door is heavy, crooked, off-track, or the spring shows a gap, leave it closed and call a professional.
toolsParts: Step stool (to reach the opener and sensors)
Soft cloth (to clean the photo-eye lenses)
Flashlight (to inspect the spring, cables, and track)
sources: Reputable DIY references (This Old House, Family Handyman, Bob Vila)
Garage-door opener manufacturer troubleshooting guides (e.g. Chamberlain/LiftMaster, Genie)
Door & Access Systems Manufacturers Association (DASMA) safety guidance on springs and cables
disclaimer: This guide is general home-maintenance information, not professional garage-door advice. Garage-door springs and cables are under high tension and can cause serious injury; when in doubt, hire a qualified garage-door technician.
verifierNote: Confirmed photo-eye misalignment as the dominant won't-close cause and the power/remote/lock-out checks as the won't-open basics. Kept torsion-spring and cable work firmly off-limits for DIY (leading injury source) and aligned cost ranges to current US pro pricing.
